sentences.text: Okay, so let's get started.
So before the break, we have mentioned that you should try to train a model with two layers and a model with six layers.
And one thing that you may have observed is that a six-layer model may not be as good as a simple model.
And this is weird because we have mentioned that deep neural network becomes powerful because we have more layers.
But now this is not the case.
So in the rest of this lecture, we are going to discuss several practical aspects in training neural network models.
So let's start with the easy thing.
Let's consider a practical scenario in our real life.
That is, I may want to predict who will repay their loan and who will default.
So in such a scenario, a lender helps you to investigate this problem and you are given the files of 100 applicants.
Among these 100 applicants, there are five of them defaulted within three years.
So your goal is to build a model and predict who else is going to default.
For such a job, what you may find is they give you some photos and they find that all the five people who defaulted worn blue shirts during their interviews.
And your model also find that blue shirts is a strong signal for default.
So whether this is a valid feature or whether there are some problem when we make this conclusion.
This is a very classic problem of overfitting or there's no codal evidence that blue shirts actually predict applicants default behavior, right?
The reason is because all of them are wearing blue shirts.
So you cannot say only those who default wear such a blue shirts.
This is one thing.
And the other thing is using human logic, we know that the color of the shirts should have no codal inference with whether he's going to default or not.
So because of that, we know that this feature may not be a valid feature.
And this introduced our classic discussion of training error and generalization error.
training error means the error on the training data set.
And generalization error refers to your error on a new data.
And we always say that the performance on the training data does not represent the performance on a new data set.
This is very similar to we make practices for a...

aligned_segments.text: Okay, so let's get started.
So before the break, we have mentioned that you should try to train a model with two layers and a model with six layers.
And one thing that you may have observed is that a six-layer model may not be as good as a simple model.
And this is weird because we have mentioned that deep neural network becomes powerful because we have more layers.
But now this is not the case.
So in the rest of this lecture, we are going to discuss several practical aspects in training neural network models.
So let's start with the easy thing.
Let's consider a practical scenario in our real life.

aligned_pages.ppt_text: Predict Who Will Repay TheirLoans •A lender hires you to investigate who will repay their  loans •You are given complete files on 100 •5 defaulted within 3 years Image creditdebt.org

aligned_segments.text: That is, I may want to predict who will repay their loan and who will default.
So in such a scenario, a lender helps you to investigate this problem and you are given the files of 100 applicants.
Among these 100 applicants, there are five of them defaulted within three years.
So your goal is to build a model and predict who else is going to default.

aligned_pages.ppt_text:
A Surprising Finding
• All 5 people who defaulted wore blue shirts during interviews
• Your model may find this strong signal as well

aligned_segments.text: For such a job, what you may find is they give you some photos and they find that all the five people who defaulted worn blue shirts during their interviews.
And your model also find that blue shirts is a strong signal for default.
So whether this is a valid feature or whether there are some problem when we make this conclusion.
This is a very classic problem of overfitting or there's no codal evidence that blue shirts actually predict applicants default behavior, right?
The reason is because all of them are wearing blue shirts.
So you cannot say only those who default wear such a blue shirts.
This is one thing.
And the other thing is using human logic, we know that the color of the shirts should have no codal inference with whether he's going to default or not.
So because of that, we know that this feature may not be a valid feature.

aligned_pages.ppt_text:
Training Error and Generalization Error
• Training error (bias): model error on the training data
• Generalization error (variance): model error on new data
• Example: practice a future exam with past exams
• Doing well on past exams (training error) doesn’t guarantee a  good score on the future exam (generalization error)
• Student A gets 0 error on past exams by rote learning
• Student B  understands the reasons for givenanswers

Okay, so let's get started.
So before the break, we have mentioned that you should try to train a model with two layers and a model with six layers.
And one thing that you may have observed is that a six-layer model may not be as good as a simple model.
And this is weird because we have mentioned that deep neural network becomes powerful because we have more layers.
But now this is not the case.
So in the rest of this lecture, we are going to discuss several practical aspects in training neural network models.
So let's start with the easy thing.
Let's consider a practical scenario in our real life.

bullets.ai_comment: This slide introduces the practical side of training neural networks, motivated by a hands-on observation: a 6-layer model unexpectedly performed worse than a simpler 2-layer model. The teacher uses this surprising result to highlight that having more layers does not automatically guarantee better performance, which seems to contradict the general intuition that deeper networks are more powerful. The rest of the lecture is dedicated to understanding why this happens and what practical techniques can address it. This topic is significant because knowing how to properly train and validate models is essential for building reliable deep learning systems in real-world applications.

generated_pages.ppt_text: Predict Who Will Repay TheirLoans •A lender hires you to investigate who will repay their  loans •You are given complete files on 100 •5 defaulted within 3 years Image creditdebt.org

aligned_segments.text: That is, I may want to predict who will repay their loan and who will default.
So in such a scenario, a lender helps you to investigate this problem and you are given the files of 100 applicants.
Among these 100 applicants, there are five of them defaulted within three years.
So your goal is to build a model and predict who else is going to default.

bullets.ai_comment: This slide introduces a classic binary classification problem in machine learning: predicting loan repayment vs. default. The teacher frames it as a real-world scenario where a lender needs an automated decision-support system. The goal is to build a predictive model using historical applicant data, which is a common application of supervised learning in the finance industry.

bullets.ppt_text: A lender hires you to investigate who will repay their  loans
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher sets up the problem as a consulting scenario: a lender (e.g., a bank) brings you in as a data scientist to investigate repayment behavior. This positions the task as applied machine learning with a clear business stakeholder and objective. Understanding who the end-user is matters because the model's output will directly influence lending decisions with real financial consequences.

bullets.ppt_text: You are given complete files on 100
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher specifies that you receive the complete files — meaning full applicant records — for 100 individuals, which serves as your training dataset. This is a notably small dataset, which the teacher implicitly highlights to underscore the challenge of building a reliable model with limited data. The completeness of the files suggests rich features (e.g., income, credit history, employment) are available for each applicant.

Out of the 100 applicants, only 5 actually defaulted within a three-year window, meaning the dataset is heavily imbalanced — 95% repaid and only 5% defaulted. The teacher emphasizes this as the labeled ground truth your model must learn from. This class imbalance is a critical challenge, as a naive model could achieve 95% accuracy simply by predicting 'repays' every time, yet completely fail at identifying the risky borrowers the lender actually cares about.

generated_pages.ppt_text:
A Surprising Finding
• All 5 people who defaulted wore blue shirts during interviews
• Your model may find this strong signal as well

aligned_segments.text: For such a job, what you may find is they give you some photos and they find that all the five people who defaulted worn blue shirts during their interviews.
And your model also find that blue shirts is a strong signal for default.
So whether this is a valid feature or whether there are some problem when we make this conclusion.
This is a very classic problem of overfitting or there's no codal evidence that blue shirts actually predict applicants default behavior, right?
The reason is because all of them are wearing blue shirts.
So you cannot say only those who default wear such a blue shirts.
This is one thing.
And the other thing is using human logic, we know that the color of the shirts should have no codal inference with whether he's going to default or not.
So because of that, we know that this feature may not be a valid feature.

bullets.ai_comment: The teacher introduces a counterintuitive example to illustrate a fundamental machine learning pitfall. The scenario involves loan default prediction where an unexpected pattern emerges from a small dataset. This sets up a discussion about why seemingly strong statistical signals can be completely meaningless in practice. The 'surprise' is that a model can confidently latch onto features that have zero causal relationship with the outcome.

bullets.ppt_text: • All 5 people who defaulted wore blue shirts during interviews
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher describes a dataset where photos of loan applicants reveal that every single one of the five people who defaulted happened to be wearing a blue shirt during their interview. This is a classic example of a spurious correlation — a pattern that exists purely by coincidence in a small sample. The teacher emphasizes that with only five defaulters, there is simply not enough data to distinguish a real signal from random noise. This highlights the danger of drawing conclusions from very small datasets.

bullets.ppt_text: • Your model may find this strong signal as well
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher warns that a machine learning model, lacking human common sense, will treat 'blue shirt' as a genuinely strong predictive feature for default because it correlates perfectly with the outcome in the training data. This is the core problem of overfitting — the model memorizes noise specific to the training set rather than learning a generalizable pattern. The teacher further points out two logical flaws: first, all defaulters wearing blue shirts does not mean only defaulters wear blue shirts; second, human logic tells us shirt color has no causal connection to financial behavior. This example underscores why domain knowledge and causal reasoning must complement statistical modeling.

Training Error and Generalization Error
• Training error (bias): model error on the training data
• Generalization error (variance): model error on new data

• Student B  understands the reasons for givenanswers

bullets.ai_comment: This slide introduces one of the most fundamental distinctions in machine learning: training error versus generalization error. The teacher frames this as a 'classic discussion,' signaling its central importance in understanding model performance. These two concepts are the foundation for diagnosing whether a model is underfitting or overfitting. Understanding the gap between them is essential for building models that work well in the real world, not just on the data they were trained on.

bullets.ppt_text: • Training error (bias): model error on the training data
bullets.ai_comment: Training error is the error a model makes on the very data it was trained on — essentially, how well the model has 'learned' its training set. The teacher defines it straightforwardly as the error on the training dataset. It is associated with the concept of bias: a high training error typically means the model is too simple to capture the underlying patterns (underfitting). Minimizing training error alone is not sufficient for a good model.

bullets.ppt_text: • Generalization error (variance): model error on new data
bullets.ai_comment: Generalization error measures how well the model performs on data it has never seen before, which is the true test of a model's usefulness. The teacher defines it as the error on new data, contrasting it directly with training error. It is linked to variance: a model that memorizes training data may have high variance and perform poorly on new inputs. This is the metric that ultimately matters in any real-world deployment.

bullets.ppt_text: • Example: practice a future exam with past exams
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher uses the analogy of a student preparing for a future exam by practicing with past exams, which directly mirrors the machine learning setup of training on historical data to perform on unseen data. This analogy makes the abstract concept very intuitive: past exams are the training set, and the future exam is the real-world test. The teacher begins to introduce this analogy around the 04:56 mark, though the full explanation continues beyond the provided transcript. It is a powerful illustration of why strong performance on known data does not guarantee success on unknown data.

bullets.ppt_text: • Doing well on past exams (training error) doesn't guarantee a  good score on the future exam (generalization error)
bullets.ai_comment: This is the core lesson of the slide: low training error does not imply low generalization error. The teacher explicitly states that 'performance on the training data does not represent the performance on a new data set,' which is the machine learning formulation of this exam analogy. A model (or student) can appear to perform perfectly on known examples while completely failing on novel ones. This gap between training and generalization error is the central problem that techniques like regularization, cross-validation, and dropout are designed to address.

bullets.ppt_text: • Student A gets 0 error on past exams by rote learning
bullets.ai_comment: Student A represents a model that overfits: by memorizing answers through rote learning, it achieves perfect performance (0 error) on the training data (past exams) without developing any true understanding. In machine learning terms, this is analogous to a model with too many parameters that memorizes noise and specific examples rather than learning general patterns. While not explicitly elaborated in the provided transcript segment, this concept is a direct extension of the teacher's analogy. Such a model will likely fail badly when faced with new, slightly different inputs.

bullets.ppt_text: • Student B  understands the reasons for givenanswers
bullets.ai_comment: Student B represents the ideal model: one that learns the underlying reasoning and patterns rather than memorizing specific examples. This kind of deep understanding allows generalization — performing well not just on seen examples but on new, unseen ones. In machine learning, this corresponds to a well-regularized model that captures true structure in the data. The contrast between Student A and Student B elegantly illustrates why generalization, not just training accuracy, is the ultimate goal.
